title: Selected Psalms — Psalm 12: Protection from Evil Speech

sections.opening:
Sit, my dear one... the candle flickers and we approach ancient wisdom. You know that some psalms speak to the heart and some psalms speak to our times. Psalm 12 is one such psalm — a sharp cry against the corruption of speech, against a world where truth disappears and evil speech reigns. King David writes from soul-deep pain: "Help, Lord, for the godly man ceases to be" — for the good people are gone. This might sound extreme, but he means something very profound: truth and honesty in speech are in danger of extinction.

This is not a psalm of despair — this is a psalm of inner revolution. David understands that the only way to restore truth to the world is to start from your place of truth. To guard your tongue, to speak only from honesty. For when you guard the tongue, you guard the entire world.
sections.theoryA:
What Does It Mean to Guard the Tongue in a World of Words?

Guarding the tongue is not just "not speaking evil" — it's the art of speaking from inner truth. Psalm 12 describes a world where "they speak with flattering lips and a double heart" — people who say one thing and mean another. The problem is not only open evil speech, but the fundamental lack of authenticity.

King David identifies three stages of speech corruption: first, "flattering lips" — sweet speech that conceals evil intentions. Second, "a boastful tongue" — pride that leads to arrogant speech. And third, conscious decision: "our lips are our own; who is lord over us?" — refusal to recognize any moral authority.

But there is hope. The central verse says: "The words of the Lord are pure words, like silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times." In other words, there is another way of speaking — speech that has been refined, that has examined itself many times before coming out. When you learn to refine your words, you become a bearer of the Lord's words.

In Kabbalah they say that the Malkhut — the Shekhinah — is revealed through holy speech. Every word that comes from your mouth in purity returns the Shekhinah to the world. Therefore this psalm is not just a personal prayer but an act of universal repair.

practice1.instruction: Take a deep breath and say with intention the verse: 'Help, Lord, for the godly man ceases to be, for the faithful disappear from among the children of men.' Feel the pain in these words — not as despair but as a cry for change. Repeat the verse three times and set the intention that your speech will be part of the repair.

sections.theoryB:
How Does Psalm 12 Protect the Tongue in Practice?

There is a special remedy for this psalm: "against evil speech and gossip, guarding the tongue." But how does it work? The secret is that the psalm does two actions at the same time: it strengthens your awareness of the power of speech, and simultaneously connects you to the energy of pure speech.

The verse "May the Lord cut off all flattering lips" doesn't speak of external punishment — it describes an inner process. When you say these words with intention, you literally ask God to help you "cut off" your superficial speech habits. It's like spiritual surgery of speech.

There is a deep connection to gematria here: the word "words" (the words of the Lord are pure words) equals 647 in gematria, and the same number is for "seven times" (purified seven times). This is no coincidence — it teaches that purity in speech requires a process of seven stages of refinement.

In Hasidic tradition they say that reading Psalm 12 before sleep protects from all the gossip that was said about you that day. The words "You, Lord, will keep us and preserve us from this generation forever" become an energetic shield around your force field.

practice2.instruction: Write on paper the words: 'The words of the Lord are pure words.' Now think of something you need to say to someone — something you've postponed or been afraid to say. Write those words again, this time after they've been 'refined' — how would you say it with complete purity?

sections.deepDive:
What's Not Written in the Books: The Psalm as Meditation for Changing Reality

Many think Psalm 12 is passive acceptance of a bad situation. But the deep secret transmitted in Hasidic tradition is completely different: this psalm is a tool for changing reality through conscious speech. When you say "Help, Lord, for the godly man ceases to be," you're not complaining — you're declaring an intention to return and be that godly person who ceased.

The Baal Shem Tov learned from this psalm a special technique: before every important conversation, he would whisper "The words of the Lord are pure words." This reminded him that his speech could be clean of personal interests and hidden motives.

There is also a deep Kabbalistic secret here: the word "generation" (keep us from this generation forever) hints at transmission between generations. Each generation inherits the speech patterns of the previous generation — the gossip, hatred, lack of trust. But whoever connects to this psalm can break the chain. Not pass on the poison in speech to their children.

Tradition tells that the Rabbi of Würzburg would give his students a special exercise: a whole week of speaking only absolute truth. Not out of religious fanaticism, but from a desire to understand what happens to the soul when you guard the tongue. The students would report that it changes their entire perception of themselves and the world.

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'The words of the Lord are pure words, like silver tried in a furnace'?
options: Speech that has undergone a process of refinement and deep examination
Words that are precious like silver and gold
Psalm verses written on silver tablets
Prayers said in a low voice
questions.explanation: The metaphor of refined silver describes a process of purifying speech — repeated examination of intentions before words leave the mouth.

questions.question: Why is Psalm 12 considered a remedy against evil speech?
options: Because it contains clear prohibitions against gossip
Because it strengthens awareness of the power of speech and connects to pure energy
Because it was written by King David who never spoke evil
Because it's short and easy to remember
questions.explanation: The psalm works on two levels: it raises awareness of the great power of speech and simultaneously connects the reader to a higher level of expression.

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'purified seven times'?
options: Seven different types of purification
A purification process based on the sacred number seven
Seven stages of examining speech before it comes out
Purification for seven days
questions.explanation: The number seven symbolizes completeness — speech that has been thoroughly examined at all possible levels before being expressed.

questions.question: How is Psalm 12 connected to repairing the world?
options: It prays for the destruction of all the wicked
Every pure word returns the Shekhinah to the world
It calls on all Jews to stop speaking
It promises speedy redemption
questions.explanation: In Kabbalistic tradition, holy speech is one of the main tools for repairing the world — returning holiness to reality.

questions.question: What was the Baal Shem Tov's technique with this psalm?
options: To say the entire psalm three times a day
To say 'The words of the Lord are pure words' before important conversations
To write the psalm on a silver amulet
To sing the psalm with a special melody
questions.explanation: The Baal Shem Tov would use this verse as a 'mantra' to remember that speech can be clean of interests and ego.

closing.summary: Psalm 12 teaches us that in a world where truth has almost disappeared, the way to restore it is to start from our place of speech. Guarding the tongue is not just avoiding gossip — it's the art of speaking from inner truth. Every word that undergoes 'refinement' before leaving the mouth returns holiness to the world.
closing.wisdom: Speak only truth, and even truth — only at the right time.
closing.nextPreview: In the next lesson we'll explore Psalm 13 — 'How long, Lord, will You forget me forever' — how to deal with feelings of abandonment and find hope within despair.

faq.question: Does Psalm 12 work only in Hebrew or also in translation?
faq.answer: The full power is in Hebrew, but the intention and awareness you bring to reading are more important than the language itself. Read in the language that speaks to your heart.

faq.question: How many times a day is it recommended to say the psalm?
faq.answer: There's no obligation — quality is more important than quantity. Once a day with full intention is better than thirteen times carelessly.

faq.question: Can the psalm help if I've already spoken evil speech?
faq.answer: Absolutely. The psalm also works as 'repair' for speech that has already come out. The intention for future purity cancels the negative energy of the past.

faq.question: What's the difference between Psalm 12 and Psalm 34 regarding guarding the tongue?
faq.answer: Psalm 12 focuses on protection from others' evil speech and purifying your own speech. Psalm 34 gives practical instructions on how to guard the tongue.

seo.title: Psalm 12 — Protection from Evil Speech and Purifying Speech
seo.description: Learn about Psalm 12 and how it protects against evil speech. Remedies, deep meanings and practical techniques for guarding the tongue and pure speech in Hasidic tradition and Kabbalah.
